source.title: Temporal difference models describe higher-order learning in humans

confidence_rationale: Excellent source quality (Nature, human fMRI, Dayan/Dolan/Friston collaboration). Mapping fidelity is moderate: the paper demonstrates a *temporal* gradient (harm prediction building up as a cue sequence unfolds before the painful event) which is the temporal analogue of ARC-024's spatial gradient. The brain tracks harm signal magnitude across the sequence of cues leading up to contact — supporting the proxy-gradient claim. Transfer risk is elevated because temporal prediction gradients and spatial proximity gradients are distinct mechanisms, even if both support the broader claim.
failure_signatures: Temporal prediction gradients (cue1 -> cue2 -> pain) are structurally analogous to but not identical with spatial proximity gradients (distance to hazard).
Ventral striatum/insula activity reflects reward-prediction-error signals, not the harm gradient field directly; the mapping to CausalGridWorldV2's hazard_field requires additional theoretical steps.
Study used electrical pain stimuli; generalisability to naturalistic harm sources is uncertain.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Neural activity in ventral striatum and anterior insula corresponds to temporal difference (TD) prediction error signals during sequential aversive conditioning: the brain generates graded harm prediction signals that update across a sequence of cues preceding the painful stimulus.
mapping.ree_translation: The TD harm prediction signal is the temporal version of ARC-024's spatial gradient: in both cases, the brain (and by extension E2/E3 in REE) represents harm as a graded signal that builds toward an asymptotic limit (the contact event). The hazard_field in CausalGridWorldV2 instantiates the spatial version; TD learning over trajectories captures the temporal version. Both support the architectural claim that harm signals are not binary at-contact.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper demonstrates temporal gradients, not spatial proximity gradients. ARC-024's primary operationalisation in the REE environment is spatial (proximity to hazard cell). The inference requires treating temporal distance and spatial distance as equivalent operalisations of the same underlying gradient structure.
mapping.source_context: Human fMRI, sequential aversive conditioning with electrical pain, healthy participants

confidence_components.notes: Highest-quality source in this batch. Mapping fidelity penalised for temporal-vs-spatial distinction. Transfer risk elevated for same reason plus pain-stimulus specificity.
